description: Explains how Craft builds chunk, block, plant, sign, sky, text, and player geometry into OpenGL buffers, then renders them with shaders, culling, daylight, fog, transparency, and ambient occlusion.
document:
Craft renders its Minecraft-like world with modern OpenGL rather than the deprecated fixed-function pipeline. The README describes the scene as simple primitives generated in code, with vertex buffer objects carrying position, normal, and texture data into vertex and fragment shaders. That shape is visible in `src/main.c`, where startup loads four textures for blocks, font text, sky, and signs, then builds separate shader programs for block surfaces, lines, text, and the sky. The small Attrib structure in the same file stores the program plus the attribute and uniform locations each render path needs, so the frame code can bind the right shader and feed it matrices, textures, time of day, camera position, and related render state.

Chunk geometry is prepared before drawing so the frame loop can be mostly buffer submission. The README notes that only exposed block faces are rendered, and `compute_chunk` implements that by looking at a chunk plus neighboring chunk maps, building an opacity volume, counting faces whose adjacent cells are transparent, and skipping fully hidden blocks. It also computes per-corner ambient occlusion and block light values from nearby cells. Those values travel in the same vertex stream as texture coordinates, so the block shader can combine texture color with daylight, local light, directional diffuse light, and the darker corners produced by occlusion. Plants use crossed rectangles instead of full cubes, and their rotation comes from simplex noise so nearby plants do not all face exactly the same way.

The primitive builders in `src/cube.c` are the source of most renderable geometry. Cube faces are emitted with positions, normals, tile-atlas coordinates, occlusion, and light; the diagonal of each quad is flipped when the occlusion pattern would otherwise shade the face less smoothly. Plant geometry uses four rectangular faces and the same ten-float vertex layout as block geometry. The sky is generated by recursively subdividing an octahedron into a sphere, and text is generated as rectangles over bitmap atlas coordinates. Buffer helpers in `src/util.c` upload these arrays to OpenGL and free the temporary CPU-side data after creating the GPU buffer.

Each frame starts by preparing player and chunk state, then draws the three-dimensional scene in a fixed order. The sky is rendered first by `render_sky`, using the player's rotation but keeping the camera position at the center of the sphere. The depth buffer is then cleared so world geometry is not blocked by the sky. `render_chunks` ensures chunks are available, builds a camera matrix with the helpers in `src/matrix.c`, extracts frustum planes, and draws only chunks inside the render radius and camera view. Sign text and other players are drawn after blocks, followed by optional wireframe selection, HUD crosshairs, the held item preview, chat and status text, and an optional picture-in-picture pass that temporarily changes the viewport and renders the scene from another player's point of view.

The shaders complete the visual treatment. The block vertex shader computes diffuse lighting, fog distance, and a vertical fog coordinate from the camera and vertex position. The block fragment shader discards magenta texture pixels for transparent cutouts, treats clouds specially in orthographic mode, adds daylight and local light, applies ambient occlusion, and blends distant fragments toward a color sampled from the sky texture. The sky fragment shader samples that same sky texture by time of day horizontally and sphere height vertically, matching the README's description of a textured sky dome whose texture coordinates encode time and elevation. Text shaders use the font or sign atlas, with sign rendering discarding white pixels while normal text keeps enough alpha to remain readable.
